systems.name: GrapheneOS
systems.summary: A privacy- and security-hardened Android distribution officially supported on Google Pixel devices. GrapheneOS ships with no Google apps or telemetry by default, offers sandboxed Google Play as an optional compatibility layer, and uniquely lets the owner re-lock the bootloader with their own signing key. Developer autonomy and app-distribution freedom are near-ideal — APKs install from any source without OS-vendor approval. Portability is limited by design to the Pixel line.
highlights: Bootloader re-lockable with a custom verified-boot key — the owner retains full control of the trust chain after flashing.
No Google apps, services or telemetry by default; connectivity checks point to GrapheneOS-run servers or can be disabled.
APK installation is unrestricted: no OS-vendor notarisation, no developer account registration required to ship software.
Sandboxed Google Play compatibility layer runs Play Services as an unprivileged, user-installable app — Google is just another app.
Network permission toggle denies any app all direct and indirect network access.
Fully open source under a permissive license; all code hosted publicly on GitHub; reproducible builds documented against the AOSP build system.
concerns: Official production builds are limited to a narrow range of Pixel devices.
Root shell is not available by default, by design — may not suit some power-user workflows.
Release binaries are signed with private keys, so bit-for-bit reproducibility requires comparing unsigned outputs.

evidence.note: Bootloader unlock and re-lock with custom key
Open-source project with code on GitHub
Public source repositories
Connectivity checks via GrapheneOS servers by default
No Google services by default; sandboxed Play optional
Reproducible build documentation
APK install unrestricted; no OS-vendor notarisation
No Google apps by default; alternative stores supported
Minimal first-party apps; Vanadium replaceable
Sandboxed Play services compatibility layer
Officially supports Pixel 6 through Pixel 10 Pro Fold

systems.name: iOS
systems.summary: Apple's proprietary mobile operating system, shipped exclusively on iPhone hardware. iOS scores well on user-facing privacy controls such as App Tracking Transparency and opt-in analytics, but scores very low across ecosystem freedom and owner sovereignty dimensions due to a fused bootloader, closed-source codebase, mandatory App Store distribution (outside the EU), mandatory developer enrollment and code signing, App Review content gating, and a WebKit-only browser engine mandate outside the EU.
highlights: App Tracking Transparency requires apps to obtain user permission before tracking across apps and websites.
Analytics and diagnostics sharing is opt-in during device setup, not enabled by default.
Security updates are delivered promptly with public CVE disclosures.
End-to-end encryption available for iMessage and optional Advanced Data Protection for iCloud.
concerns: Bootloader is fused; no user-accessible unlock, root, or custom verified-boot key path.
Fully proprietary, closed-source OS; modification and redistribution prohibited by license.
App Store is the sole distribution channel globally; EU DMA alternative marketplaces still require Apple notarisation and fees.
Apple Developer Program enrollment ($99/yr), code signing, and App Review are mandatory for all distribution.
WebKit-only browser engine mandate applies globally outside the EU; alternative engines permitted only in EU since iOS 17.4.
NFC payment access restricted to Apple Pay globally; third-party HCE access opened only in the EU under DMA.
iOS runs exclusively on Apple-designed iPhone hardware; no portability to other devices.

evidence.note: Proprietary license; no modification or reverse engineering
Licensed Application EULA prohibits redistribution
Analytics sharing is opt-in during setup
App Tracking Transparency and privacy controls
Device analytics data types disclosed
Developer Program enrollment and code signing required
Core Technology Fee of EUR 0.50 per install in EU
App Review gates all App Store distribution
EU alt-marketplaces require Apple notarisation
WebKit-only mandate lifted only in EU since iOS 17.4

systems.name: Android (Stock on Pixel)
systems.summary: Google's flagship Android experience on Pixel hardware, shipping Android 16 with the full Google Mobile Services suite. The OS permits bootloader unlocking and APK installation from third-party sources, but layers significant friction through Play Protect warnings, Play Integrity attestation, and pre-installed Google apps that cannot be fully uninstalled. Privacy defaults favor data collection, and core GMS components are closed-source despite the AOSP base being Apache 2.0 licensed.
highlights: Bootloader can be unlocked via Developer Options and fastboot on most Pixel models.
AOSP base is Apache 2.0 licensed with source publicly available and buildable.
Developers can distribute APKs directly to users without OS-level notarization or approval.
Default browser, keyboard, launcher, and most default apps can be replaced by the user.
Monthly Android Security Bulletins provide transparent disclosure of patched vulnerabilities.
Google Takeout allows export of account data in standard formats.
concerns: Google Mobile Services (Play Services, Play Store, Chrome, Gmail, YouTube, Maps, Drive, Photos) are closed-source and pre-installed; most cannot be fully uninstalled, only disabled.
Play Protect uses loaded terminology ('harmful app', 'blocked by Play Protect') and enhanced fraud protection to discourage or block installs from outside Google Play in 185 markets.
Play Integrity API enables app developers to detect and block sideloaded copies via remediation dialogs that redirect users to Google Play.
Telemetry data is transmitted before device setup completes; usage and diagnostics collection is opt-out rather than opt-in.
Bootloader unlock wipes all data and causes Play Integrity to report a non-standard device state, which can break banking and other attestation-dependent apps.
Pixel hardware is the only supported target for this OS configuration, limiting portability.

evidence.note: Bootloader unlock via fastboot on Android devices
AOSP licensed under Apache 2.0; GMS is proprietary
AOSP source publicly available and buildable
Pixel usage and diagnostics sharing settings
Verified Boot chain-of-trust documentation
Play Integrity verdicts flag sideloaded apps
Play Protect scans and warns on non-Play installs
Enhanced fraud protection expanded to 185 markets
DMA browser and search choice screen in EEA
Pre-installed system apps can be disabled, not removed
Documented public APIs via Android SDK

systems.name: Samsung One UI
systems.description: Samsung's Android skin on Galaxy devices, layering Samsung services on top of the Google stack.

systems.name: HarmonyOS
systems.summary: Huawei's proprietary mobile operating system, built on the open-source OpenHarmony foundation but shipped as a fully closed commercial product on Huawei devices in the Chinese market. HarmonyOS NEXT (5.0) abandoned AOSP and Android app compatibility in favor of a native HarmonyOS ecosystem centered on AppGallery and Huawei Mobile Services. The OS scores very low across sovereignty and ecosystem freedom dimensions due to a permanently locked bootloader, closed-source proprietary codebase, mandatory Huawei ID with real-name authentication, no consumer sideloading of apps, and deep integration of Huawei first-party services with no documented path to full replacement.
highlights: OpenHarmony upstream is open source under Apache 2.0, governed by the OpenAtom Foundation.
POSIX compatibility layer enables some cross-platform interoperability for developers.
Developer account registration for AppGallery Connect appears to be free, unlike Apple's $99/yr program.
Star Shield security architecture provides structured permission controls and encrypted sharing.
concerns: Huawei closed its official bootloader unlock service in 2018; no unlock path exists for HarmonyOS NEXT devices.
HarmonyOS NEXT is fully proprietary and closed-source; the consumer OS cannot be built from source or redistributed.
No consumer sideloading of HAP packages; AppGallery is the sole distribution channel for end users.
Huawei ID with real-name authentication is required for device setup and app installation in the Chinese market.
Default browser, search, cloud, assistant (Celia), and maps are all Huawei services with no documented full replacement path.
Runs exclusively on Huawei-branded hardware; not installable on third-party devices.

evidence.note: Huawei ceased bootloader unlock codes in 2018
No known root or bootloader unlock for NEXT
OpenHarmony is Apache 2.0 under OpenAtom Foundation
HarmonyOS NEXT is proprietary built on OpenHarmony
OpenHarmony source public on GitCode since 2025
AppGallery requires developer registration and review
Enterprise-only internal distribution like Apple model
Celia, AppGallery, HMS Core as default services
POSIX layer and documented API levels 12-18
Runs on Mate 70/80, Pura, nova Huawei devices only

systems.name: LineageOS
systems.summary: A community-maintained, fully open-source Android distribution that ships without Google Mobile Services by default and supports hundreds of devices across multiple vendors. LineageOS scores strongly on software freedom, developer autonomy, and app distribution freedom, with all source publicly available under Apache 2.0 and no OS-vendor gatekeeping of app installation. The main limitations are the lack of first-class bootloader re-lock with custom verified-boot keys and the absence of reproducible builds or formal security audits.
highlights: Fully open-source under Apache 2.0; all code public on GitHub and Gerrit with documented per-device build instructions.
No Google apps or services by default; no mandatory telemetry; device statistics are opt-in only.
APK installation unrestricted with no OS-vendor notarisation, developer account, or fees required.
Officially supports devices from Pixel, OnePlus, Xiaomi, Samsung, Motorola, Fairphone, Nothing, and other vendors.
All default apps (launcher, browser, dialer, contacts) are replaceable with no vendor steering or UI nudges.
Trust interface centralises privacy and security settings for user visibility.
concerns: Bootloader re-lock with custom verified-boot keys is not a documented first-class feature; LineageOS FAQ warns against relocking on most devices.
Proprietary vendor blobs (modem, firmware, HALs) are required for hardware support and cannot be audited.
No reproducible build infrastructure and no formal third-party security audit of LineageOS specifically.
Root access is not available by default; users must flash a separate tool such as Magisk.

evidence.note: FAQ warns against relocking; bootloader unlock required
Engineering post on firmware chain of trust
Public source manifest repo; Apache 2.0 licensed
Per-device build-from-source instructions on wiki
Opt-in device statistics; anonymised ID resets on wipe
Google apps are optional, not included by default
Public Gerrit code review for all changes
Technical changelogs describe actual code changes
No OS-vendor notarisation or app approval required
No Play Protect; third-party stores work natively
Trust interface; all default apps are replaceable
Full AOSP API compatibility; open Android SDK
Hundreds of supported devices across many vendors

systems.name: Windows 11
systems.summary: Microsoft's flagship desktop OS, ubiquitous on consumer and enterprise PCs and built around a Microsoft-account-centric experience. Windows 11 preserves the historic strengths of the PC — developers can still ship unsigned executables, users can install from any source — but wraps that permissiveness in aggressive vendor steering, mandatory Microsoft-account setup, and a minimum telemetry level that cannot be disabled on Home or Pro. Proprietary license, closed source and visible steering toward Edge, Bing and OneDrive produce the low marks on freedom, privacy and device neutrality.
highlights: No mandatory OS-vendor notarisation: unsigned .exe files still run (with a bypassable SmartScreen warning).
Win32 API surface is documented and open to third-party developers at no cost.
Third-party app stores and package managers (Chocolatey, Scoop, Winget) work natively.
Secure Boot can be disabled in UEFI firmware by the device owner.
Administrator access and an unrestricted shell are available by default.
concerns: Home and Pro editions cannot disable Windows diagnostic data entirely; "Required" is the minimum and default.
Initial setup of Windows 11 Home and Pro requires an internet connection and a Microsoft account; Microsoft is actively removing documented bypasses.
SmartScreen uses loaded terminology ("unknown publisher", "Windows protected your PC") to discourage third-party installations.
Aggressive UI steering: Edge re-nags after default-browser changes; Bing is the default search; OneDrive auto-enrolls.
Proprietary, closed-source license; the OS cannot be built from source or forked.
Installation requires TPM 2.0 and UEFI Secure Boot-capable hardware, excluding many older PCs.

evidence.note: TPM 2.0 and UEFI Secure Boot required
Diagnostic data off only on Enterprise/Education
Microsoft account and internet required at setup
Win32 API open; unsigned executables run
SmartScreen warns unknown publishers but is bypassable
Microsoft removing local-account bypass methods
Win32 API publicly documented
Strict hardware requirements exclude older PCs

systems.name: macOS Tahoe
systems.summary: Apple's proprietary desktop operating system, shipped exclusively on Apple-branded hardware and integrated with iCloud and the Mac App Store. macOS Tahoe preserves meaningful owner authority through Recovery-accessible security downgrades and a real Unix shell, and its privacy posture is above average with opt-in analytics and no mandatory telemetry baseline. However, the proprietary license prohibiting redistribution and non-Apple hardware installation, mandatory notarisation for frictionless app distribution, heavy first-party service steering, and strict hardware binding produce a low overall sovereignty score.
highlights: Permissive Security mode in Recovery allows disabling SIP, loading third-party kexts, and booting custom XNU kernels on Apple Silicon.
Analytics and diagnostics sharing is opt-in; no mandatory telemetry baseline unlike some competing desktop operating systems.
Darwin kernel (XNU) and core userland components are published as open source under the Apple Public Source License.
Third-party browser engines (Gecko, Blink) run natively on macOS, unlike iOS which mandates WebKit.
Default browser and default email client are user-replaceable through System Settings.
App Tracking Transparency framework requires apps to request permission before tracking across apps and websites.
concerns: EULA prohibits installation on non-Apple-branded hardware, redistribution, and derivative works, binding the OS to a single vendor's hardware.
Notarisation via the Apple Developer Program ($99/year) is effectively required for frictionless distribution outside the Mac App Store; Apple can revoke Developer ID certificates.
Gatekeeper displays scare dialogs for unnotarised software stating Apple could not verify it is free of malware, creating friction for legitimate non-notarised apps.
Spotlight, Siri, Finder, and iCloud are deeply integrated with no full replacement path; iCloud is enabled by default for several features.
No reproducible builds, no public build infrastructure, and XNU source releases are published with variable delays after OS release.

evidence.note: Permissive Security allows custom kernels and SIP disable
SIP can be disabled via csrutil in Recovery
Darwin and XNU source published under APSL
EULA prohibits non-Apple hardware and redistribution
Analytics sharing is opt-in via Privacy settings
Apple privacy policy describes data handling practices
Security release notes published per update
Notarisation required for Developer ID distribution
Developer Program costs $99/year for signing
Users can change default browser and email app
macOS Tahoe product page showing integrated services
macOS 26 release notes and documented APIs
License restricts installation to Apple hardware only

systems.name: ChromeOS
systems.summary: Google's cloud-first desktop operating system, shipped on Google-certified Chromebook hardware and centered on the Chrome browser and Google account ecosystem. ChromeOS scores respectably on web-standards interoperability thanks to its Chromium base, but the mandatory Google account for setup, irremovable Chrome browser as the sole system web handler, Google-gated app distribution through the Chrome Web Store and Play Store, and restriction to certified hardware produce a low sovereignty score. Developer Mode provides an escape hatch for advanced users but wipes user data on entry and displays a persistent warning screen.
highlights: ChromiumOS upstream is open-source under a BSD-style license with public source and a documented build-from-source path.
Developer Mode grants root shell access and permits booting non-Google-signed images on owned hardware.
Linux on ChromeOS (Crostini) provides a Debian container where users can install arbitrary Linux software without Play Store or Web Store gating.
Strong web-standards compliance via the Chromium engine; documented extension and Android APIs available to developers.
concerns: Google account is effectively required for device setup; Guest mode is severely limited and does not persist data.
Chrome cannot be removed or replaced as the system browser; all system-level web links route exclusively through Chrome.
Developer Mode wipes all user data on entry and displays an 'OS verification is OFF' warning on every boot; no custom verified-boot keys without defeating hardware write protection.
Primary app distribution channels (Chrome Web Store, Google Play Store) are Google-gated; Android APK sideloading requires enabling Linux or Developer Mode.
ChromeOS runs only on Google-certified Chromebook hardware; ChromeOS Flex for generic x86 PCs is a separate product.

evidence.note: Developer Mode entry wipes data, shows warning each boot
Verified Boot uses Google root-of-trust; no user keys
ChromiumOS packages use BSD-Google and FOSS licenses
ChromiumOS developer guide for building from source
Chromebook setup requires Google account sign-in
Chrome Privacy Whitepaper describes data collection
ChromeOS enterprise release notes published per version
Linux on ChromeOS allows unrestricted app installs in container
Android apps install via Play Store on supported models
Chrome is the integrated browser; cannot be replaced
Chrome 146 release notes document web platform APIs
ChromeOS Flex is separate; ChromeOS requires Chromebooks

systems.name: Ubuntu
systems.summary: A Canonical-backed, Debian-derived Linux distribution and one of the most widely used desktop Linux platforms. Ubuntu ships a FOSS core with user-controlled boot, root access, opt-in telemetry, and no required vendor account, scoring well on owner sovereignty and developer autonomy. Scores are moderated by the proprietary Snap Store backend, Firefox and Chromium being distributed exclusively as Snaps in official repositories, Ubuntu Pro upsell messages in apt and MOTD, and Flathub not being pre-configured by default.
highlights: GRUB is user-controlled; MokManager allows enrollment of custom Secure Boot keys alongside the Microsoft-signed Shim chain.
Main and Universe repositories are entirely free software under DFSG-aligned licenses; source available via apt and Launchpad.
Telemetry via ubuntu-report is opt-in during installation; no vendor account is required for install or daily use.
Developers can distribute .deb packages, PPAs, Flatpaks, or AppImages without OS-vendor notarisation or fees.
Six supported architectures (amd64, arm64, armhf, ppc64el, riscv64, s390x) with no hardware-ID binding or activation system.
All packages are built on the public Launchpad build infrastructure with per-package changelogs and security advisories.
concerns: The Snap Store server backend is proprietary and operated exclusively by Canonical; no federated or self-hosted alternative is officially supported.
Firefox and Chromium are distributed only as Snaps in official Ubuntu repositories since 22.04; the .deb package is a wrapper that installs the Snap.
Ubuntu Pro upsell messages appear in apt output and MOTD on LTS systems via the pre-installed ubuntu-advantage-tools package.
Flathub is not pre-configured by default; the Snap Store is the privileged app distribution channel.
The Restricted repository, enabled by default, contains non-free firmware and drivers.

evidence.note: Shim, GRUB, and MokManager key enrollment documented
Secure Boot architecture and user key management
Main is free software; Multiverse is non-free opt-in
Source packages built publicly on Launchpad
Ubuntu telemetry is opt-in; no mandatory collection
Ubuntu Pro apt messages and how to disable them
Public build infrastructure on Launchpad
Public security advisories with CVE references
Free Ubuntu One account for Snap publishing
Snap publishing is free with no mandatory fees
Firefox .deb replaced by snap-installing wrapper
Snap Store backend is proprietary; workarounds exist
Ubuntu Pro upsell in apt and MOTD on LTS systems
Ubuntu 24.04 LTS release notes and documented APIs
Six architectures: amd64, arm64, armhf, ppc64el, riscv64, s390x

systems.name: Fedora Workstation
systems.summary: A Red Hat-sponsored, community-governed Linux distribution with a leading-edge release cadence and a strict free-software orientation. Fedora Workstation ships with no default telemetry, no forced accounts, user-controlled boot and root, and an open build infrastructure on Koji. Developer autonomy is near-ideal — anyone can ship RPMs or Flatpaks, no OS-vendor notarisation, no fees — and GNOME Software treats first- and third-party sources as equals. The score is pulled down modestly by reliance on the Microsoft-signed Shim for Secure Boot and by a narrower architecture breadth than community-only distributions.
highlights: Fully free and open-source by design; third-party proprietary repositories are off by default.
No default telemetry; optional metrics are opt-in and off until the user explicitly enables them.
No user account or internet connection is required to install; installation works offline.
Developers can ship RPM packages, Flatpaks or AppImages without OS-vendor notarisation, approval or fees.
GNOME Software treats Flathub and Fedora RPM repositories as equal sources, without vendor steering.
Every package is built on the public Koji infrastructure from publicly available source RPMs.
concerns: Secure Boot relies on the Microsoft-signed Shim bootloader; replacing the trust chain with user-owned keys is possible but not straightforward.
Official architecture support (x86_64 and aarch64) is narrower than fully community-governed distributions.
Red Hat (IBM) corporate sponsorship shapes roadmap prioritisation, though governance sits with the Fedora Council.

evidence.note: User-controlled GRUB2 and sudo; standard Linux model
Described as 100% Free and Open Source
All packages must meet Fedora licensing guidelines
Opt-in metrics, off by default at setup
Public package build system and logs
Open package ecosystem; RPM and Flatpak supported
GNOME Software shows Flathub and RPMs equally
GNOME default; no preinstalled ads or vendor steering
Standard Linux stack; Flatpak and RPM integration
Official Workstation builds for x86_64 and aarch64

systems.name: Arch Linux
systems.summary: A community-governed, rolling-release Linux distribution in which the user assembles the entire system from the ground up. Arch Linux scores near the top of the rubric on owner authority, privacy, developer autonomy, app distribution freedom, and device neutrality because it ships no telemetry, requires no accounts, imposes no vendor approval on software distribution, and treats all package sources equally. The score is held back by the inclusion of some proprietary packages in the official repositories (unlike strictly FOSS-only distributions) and by official support limited to x86_64 alone.
highlights: No telemetry, no vendor account, no internet connection required to install; pkgstats is a separate opt-in package.
Full root access from the start; user chooses bootloader, kernel, init, and every component.
AUR allows any user to publish build scripts; makepkg builds from source without OS-vendor approval or fees.
No scare dialogs, no sideloading terminology, no capability degradation for software installed outside official repos.
Arch Build System and all PKGBUILDs are publicly hosted on GitLab; every official package is buildable from source.
Secure Boot configurable with user-enrolled keys via sbctl or manual MOK enrollment.
concerns: Official repositories include some proprietary packages (e.g., NVIDIA drivers, firmware blobs); Arch is pragmatic, not strictly FOSS-only.
Only x86_64 is officially supported; Arch Linux ARM is a separate, unofficial project.
Reproducible builds are not systematically verified; no project-wide reproducibility infrastructure exists.
Secure Boot out of the box requires the Microsoft-signed shim or user key enrollment; no turnkey solution.

evidence.note: User installs bootloader, kernel, root access from start
Secure Boot with user-enrolled keys via sbctl
Pragmatic; some non-free packages in official repos
All official PKGBUILDs public; build from source
No OS-level telemetry; pkgstats is opt-in
All packaging sources hosted on public GitLab
Any user can submit PKGBUILDs; no approval gate
Build and distribute packages without vendor signing
No scare dialogs; all repos treated equally
No defaults; user selects every component
Standard Linux stack; open formats throughout
Only x86_64 officially supported

systems.name: Debian Stable
systems.description: Long-running, community-governed Linux distribution known for its conservative release policy and the Debian Free Software Guidelines.

systems.name: Murena /e/OS
systems.summary: A LineageOS-based Android fork developed by the non-profit e Foundation and commercialised by Murena that ships de-Googled by default, with microG replacing Google Mobile Services and Murena Find replacing Google Search. Advanced Privacy blocks in-app trackers, masks the IP, and spoofs GPS location out of the box, and App Lounge aggregates Google Play, F-Droid, and Progressive Web Apps with a transparent privacy score per listing. Main limitations mirror LineageOS: bootloader re-lock with user-controlled verified-boot keys is device-dependent rather than a first-class feature, and /e/OS does not ship reproducible builds or publish a formal third-party security audit.
highlights: Fully open source under GPLv3+; all source on public GitLab with documented build instructions for official, unofficial and community builds.
Ships de-Googled by default: microG replaces Google Mobile Services and connectivity checks do not use Google servers.
Advanced Privacy blocks trackers from Exodus and Adaway lists by default, can spoof GPS location and hide the real IP address, and issues weekly privacy reports.
App Lounge aggregates Google Play, F-Droid and Progressive Web Apps; anonymous Play Store access works without a Google account for free apps.
Officially supports 287 smartphones from Google, Samsung, Fairphone, Motorola, Xiaomi, OnePlus, Sony and others.
Easy Installer flashes supported devices from a web browser over WebUSB without command-line tools.
concerns: Bootloader re-lock with user-provided verified-boot keys is device-dependent (e.g. Fairphone 4, some Pixels) rather than documented as a first-class /e/OS feature.
Root access is not shipped on user builds; users must switch to a community build or flash Magisk separately.
No reproducible build infrastructure and no formal third-party security audit of /e/OS itself.
Proprietary vendor blobs (firmware, modem, HALs) are inherited per device and cannot be audited.
Paid apps in App Lounge still require linking a Google account from another device with Google Play installed.

evidence.note: Root not in user builds; Magisk path documented
Public source repo; GPLv3+ licensed
Official per-device build-from-source instructions
microG and non-Google connectivity checks by default
Advanced Privacy blocks trackers, IP, location
Public GitLab; all changes as merge requests
Standard Android install; no OS-vendor approval
Play, F-Droid and PWA aggregated; anonymous Play access
Google search removed; defaults replaceable
AOSP-compatible APIs; microG compat layer
287 officially supported devices across many vendors
WebUSB Easy Installer for supported devices
